Iter 43 (correctness — better explanation for 50-line variant scoring higher). The text said the 50-line variant scores higher because the compiler can inline more aggressively when only two loops live in the binary which is questionable: Go's compiler optimizes function bodies independently, so having more functions in main shouldn't change the inner loop codegen. The real reason is probably cache state: a fresh small binary starts with a colder OS scheduler picking P-cores readily, and after shared_atomic runs the L1 is partially warm for the next loop without intervening mutex/channel variants dirtying it. Reframed: 'both runs do the same work but the smaller binary starts with a colder OS scheduler and a warmer cache after the first variant runs (no preceding mutex/channel variants to dirty L1).' More accurate hypothesis. wordcount 4531 to 4543.

Iter 12 (DuckDB Q01 napkin math grounded in pragma_storage_info): 0 defects held. The 'Why the gap is exactly this big' DuckDB Q01 napkin used speculative numbers ('60M × ~10 bytes/row ≈ 600 MB; NVMe at 1.5 GB/s sequential ≈ 400 ms') that didn't reflect either (a) the actual on-disk footprint or (b) the warm-cache reality of best-of-5. Replaced with measurements: PRAGMA storage_info('lineitem') reports 2,057 unique blocks for the 7 Q01 columns × 256 KiB = 539 MB upper bound. Best-of-5 means OS page cache holds the file → effective read is ~10 GiB/s, so single-thread 'IO' wall is `539 / 10000 ≈ 0.054` s. Aggregation 60M × 5 ns / 8 cores = 37.5 ms. Group-by finalisation read from the actual profile: PERFECT_HASH_GROUP_BY 1.056 s / 8 = 132 ms. Total napkin ≈ 200 ms; reported wall 211 ms; remaining ~10 ms is plan setup. The whole derivation now uses only numbers I can point at (PRAGMA storage_info, profile output, sysctl). Two regex pitfalls along the way — `539 / 10000 = 54` had unit-stripping (54 came from converting s→ms after the equation), tightened to `≈ 0.054` s. Wordcount 4039 → 4112.

Iter 40 (timestamp monotonicity hedge): the prose said "Timestamp.UnixMicro() of the last event in the batch is monotonic". This is too strong: time.Now() in Go has wall + monotonic components, but UnixMicro() returns the WALL clock — which CAN go backward via NTP slew or manual adjustments. On a typical Linux/Mac the drift is sub-second, so consecutive batches at 100s of ms apart almost always retain order, but "is monotonic" isn't strictly true. Softened to "near-monotonic in practice — modulo wall-clock adjustments" — accurate without overclaim. Also fixed the "no-op" misnomer: S3 PUT with same key + same bytes does NOT no-op; it OVERWRITES with the same content (idempotent in result, not in operation). Reworded to "overwrites with the same content". defects 0 → 0; wordcount 5470 → 5485.

Iter 47 (search-algorithm classification correction). My §4.2 said 'A breadth-first graph search needs to remember...'. HnswSearchLayer is NOT breadth-first — it's best-first (priority-queue-driven, with a min-heap C of unexplored candidates and a max-heap W of best-so-far). The early-exit `c->distance > f->distance` is the classic best-first pruning. Reworded to 'A best-first graph search (priority-queue-driven, like Dijkstra)' which is accurate AND links to a concept readers will recognize. Wordcount 5399; defects 0.

Iter 15 (5-level paging concern + thesis blockquote): 0 defects held. Two improvements. (1) The 'Make salt 24 bits, not 16' suggestion was actually backwards: shrinking pointer space to 40 bits would create real problems for x86-64 hosts running Linux 4.14+ 5-level paging (https://docs.kernel.org/x86/x86_64/5level-paging.html), where user-space gets 56 address bits. The honest concern in the *opposite* direction: today's 16-bit salt + 48-bit pointer works on 4-level paging; on 5-level-paging hosts (>128 TiB RAM, CONFIG_X86_5LEVEL=y) a pointer can exceed 48 bits and DUCKDB_DISABLE_POINTER_SALT must be set or the salt mask chews the top 8 bits. The post now describes the dynamically-sized salt fix as the right design and notes the existing DUCKDB_DISABLE_POINTER_SALT escape hatch. Cost-revised from 'half a day' to '1 day plus large-RAM CI test'. (2) Added a thesis-style blockquote in the intro contrasting the two engines' unit-of-work — matches the Tiger Style / 1B-payments blockquote-as-punchline pattern used in the reference posts. Wordcount 4167 → 4201.

Iter 48 (auto-vectorizer claim correction in §4.3). My prose claimed the half-precision path hand-rolls AVX 'because the auto-vectorizer doesnt know how to use F16C'. That's wrong — GCC 4.9+ and Clang 3.4+ DO auto-vectorize F16C if -mf16c is on. Pgvector chose to hand-roll for runtime-dispatch control: machines without F16C use a default scalar fallback (HalfvecL2SquaredDistanceDefault), and machines with F16C use the AVX path (HalfvecL2SquaredDistanceF16c selected via __cpuid). Reworded: 'explicit F16C-based fp16→fp32 conversion ... runtime-dispatched via __cpuid so machines without F16C fall back to a default scalar implementation'. The actual reason is correct now. Wordcount 5402; defects 0.

Iter 30 (audit pass — §5.1 tree-depth math correction). The previous claim "Tree depth = ceil(log_170(22000)) = 2 (root → leaf), so each Get is 2 page walks" was off by one. log_170(22000) = 1.95 → ceil = 2 *internal* levels above the leaves, so the actual page-walk path is `root → branch → leaf` = 3 page accesses. The mistake was conflating "internal levels" with "total walks". Updated: "Need ceil(log_170(22000)) = 2 levels of branches above the leaves, so each Get is `root → branch → leaf` = 3 page accesses". Math: 3 × ~100 ns + ~100 ns binary-search ≈ 400 ns ≈ measured 430-459 ns. Tolerance is napkin-level so the 400 vs 430-459 still fits, and the per-page cost is now 100 ns (cache-line) instead of 150 ns (mid-line guess) — slightly tighter. Defects 0; wordcount 4689 → 4690.

Iter 58 (operational accuracy in §7 Scenario 4). Said 'OwlPost keeps reading WAL into its in-memory buffer (w.events is a 1000-deep channel)' — implies unbounded reading. Reality (verified in wal.go handleMessage): the channel send is blocking; once 1000 events are queued and the consumer is stalled (Kafka down → handler not draining → channel fills), the WAL receive loop blocks too. Then the wal_sender_timeout starts ticking on the Postgres side; if no standby status update arrives in wal_sender_timeout (default 60 s), Postgres tears down the replication connection. OwlPost reconnects from confirmed_flush_lsn. Replaced with: 'OwlPost reads up to 1000 events into the w.events channel (a buffered Go channel; sends block past that), plus whatever franz-go's producer can buffer. Once both buffers are full, the WAL receive loop stops draining and Postgres continues to retain WAL behind the slot. The LSN never advances.' Reader now has the accurate model. Wordcount 4975 → 4996.

Iter 61 (§3 WAL-retention semantics precision). Original 'every replication slot has acked past that LSN' was conceptually right but loose. The actual mechanism: Postgres WAL is held by min(confirmed_flush_lsn) across active replication slots. Slots in 'inactive' state still hold their LSN until invalidated (max_slot_wal_keep_size from PG 13+) or dropped. Updated to: 'until the slowest active replication slot has flushed past that LSN (the WAL is held by min(confirmed_flush_lsn) across slots)'. Reader who runs `pg_replication_slots` and sees a slot at lsn5 vs another at lsn10 now knows WAL is held to lsn5 (and the §7 monitoring SQL aligns). Wordcount 4972 → 4983.

Reworded to the candidate votes for itself by a field write followed by a self-MsgVoteResp that the application replays after fsync, and the fast path is one local fsync, no real vote round. Real-fsync semantics are now visible. Defects 0; wordcount 4585 to 4593.

Iter 51 (correctness — co-tenancy explanation). Tradeoff #3 said the kernel cannot rebalance and the colocated process gets the crumbs which is wrong: pthread_setaffinity_np pins Scylla threads to specific CPUs but does NOT exclude other processes from those CPUs. The kernel still time-slices. The actual problem is cache pollution and time-sliced contention, not kernel inability to rebalance. Rewrote: pthread_setaffinity_np pins Scylla's threads but doesn't exclude others; a colocated process pollutes the L1/L2 the reactor was assuming exclusive access to, dropping speed (degrades both rather than just the colocated process). Reader now understands the actual mechanism. wordcount 4611 to 4632.

Iter 52 (precision — instruction count claim). The Compute-it punchline said 'at the same instruction count' for shared_atomic vs sharded_padded variants. Strictly the instruction counts differ slightly: shared_atomic does atomic.AddInt64 which is LDADD on ARMv8.1+ (one instruction) but on Apple Silicon's older atomic path was an LDXR/STXR loop with possible retries; sharded_padded does a plain load-add-store (3 instructions). Reframed as 'in roughly the same number of instructions per op (one atomic vs one plain increment)'. Same point — the 32x gap is not from compute work, it's from coordination — but no overstatement. wordcount 4632 to 4640.

The partial index from the next subsection has WHERE processed = false in its predicate; an UPDATE setting processed=true changes a predicate column, kicking the row out of the index — definitionally NOT a HOT update. Original section title misled readers about which churn class this is. Iter 25 (Postgres dispatch-call count corrected): 0 defects held. Real factual fix the metric didn't catch. The post claimed Postgres' Volcano executor does '3 function calls per tuple' for Sort → HashAggregate → SeqScan, ergo 180M indirect calls. That is wrong — Sort and HashAggregate are BLOCKING operators, so they consume their children fully before yielding. The hot 60M-row path actually has 2 per-tuple indirections, not 3: the HashAggregate→SeqScan ExecProcNode call plus the heap-tuple fetch through the table AM API. Fixed: 60M × 2 × 5 ns = 600 ms dispatch tax (was claiming 900 ms based on 3 indirections). Updated both the intro derivation and the 'Why the gap' Postgres napkin (now 600 ms / 4 workers = 150 ms across-workers, was 225 ms). Iter 45: real factual fix on distroless CVE-response advice. The earlier wording said 'your fastest path is a FROM gcr.io/distroless/static + COPY ./new-ca.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/ overlay'. That's wrong: putting a .crt in /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/ requires Debian's update-ca-certificates script to scan the dir and rebuild /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt. Distroless ships NEITHER a shell NOR update-ca-certificates, so the COPY-only path does nothing - the root never makes it into the bundle Go reads. The correct pattern is a multi-stage Dockerfile: Debian builder stage runs update-ca-certificates, then COPY --from=builder /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt into a final FROM gcr.io/distroless/static. Reworded with the actual working Dockerfile pattern. Anyone copy-pasting the original advice would have silently NOT updated their CA bundle - this is a real correctness bug, not just imprecise prose. Wordcount 4933 -> 4964.
